title: Tenant Consolidation for Cost Efficiency
description: Prefer shared resources (shared DB, shared compute pools) to reduce per-tenant overhead; enforce isolation via RLS and quotas.

cost.costNotes: Estimates are intentionally conservative; verify against current pricing before committing.

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Multi-tenant consolidation involves shared infrastructure costs. Minimum represents a small-scale deployment with shared database (RDS t3.small ~$30), shared compute pool (ECS/K8s on t3.medium instances ~$50), and basic monitoring/quotas (~$20). Maximum represents production-scale with high-availability shared database cluster (RDS m5.xlarge Multi-AZ ~$500-800), shared compute pool with auto-scaling (multiple instances ~$2000-3000), advanced monitoring and tenant isolation tools (~$500), data transfer and storage (~$500), and reserved capacity for peak loads (~$1000-1500). Cost efficiency comes from amortizing these resources across multiple tenants rather than per-tenant dedicated infrastructure. Note: actual savings depend on tenant density - higher tenant count reduces per-tenant cost significantly.
provenance.adoptionCost: High-medium complexity due to architectural re-design required. Implementation involves: (1) Designing and implementing Row-Level Security (RLS) policies in database (~$800 - requires deep database security expertise), (2) Building tenant isolation mechanisms in application layer (~$1000 - context propagation, middleware, filters), (3) Implementing quota management and enforcement systems (~$800 - rate limiting, resource tracking per tenant), (4) Refactoring existing code to be tenant-aware (~$500 - adding tenant_id columns, updating queries), (5) Testing isolation boundaries and security guarantees (~$400 - critical security testing). Requires expertise in database security, multi-tenancy patterns, and infrastructure optimization. Teams with existing multi-tenant experience can reduce this by 30-40%.
provenance.licenseCost: No direct licensing costs as this is an architectural pattern implemented using standard cloud infrastructure. RLS is native to PostgreSQL and most modern databases. Quota management can be built with open-source tools (Redis for rate limiting, Prometheus for monitoring). Commercial tenant management platforms exist (e.g., Frontegg, PropelAuth) but are optional and would add $200-2000/month to operational costs rather than one-time license fees. Core pattern is implementable entirely with open-source and cloud-native tools.

provides.reasoning: This pattern explicitly optimizes for cost efficiency in multi-tenant architectures by consolidating resources rather than isolating them per tenant. This is the core value proposition.
The pattern description explicitly mentions enforcing isolation via quotas, which is essential for preventing noisy neighbor problems in shared resource environments.
This pattern is fundamentally about implementing a specific multi-tenant architecture strategy through resource consolidation and logical isolation mechanisms.
The pattern provides logical isolation through RLS and quotas in a consolidated environment, ensuring tenant data and resource separation even within shared infrastructure.
Multi-tenant consolidation uses RLS as its primary isolation mechanism — shared infrastructure with per-tenant data visibility enforced at the database layer.
Consolidation patterns require robust tenant identification at every layer — API, application, and database — to prevent cross-tenant data leakage.

requires.reasoning: The pattern explicitly mentions using RLS (row-level security) for tenant isolation in shared databases. This is a fundamental requirement to safely implement this consolidation pattern.
The pattern requires the ability to create and manage shared resource pools (compute pools, databases) that can be allocated across multiple tenants.
To enforce RLS and quotas in a shared environment, the system must be able to identify which tenant each request or data row belongs to.
Tracking per-tenant resource usage is critical for both quota enforcement and cost allocation in consolidated environments, ensuring fair resource distribution and accurate billing.

supports_nfr.reason: Shared database architecture with consolidated resources limits maximum availability to three nines due to single points of failure.
Row-level security policy evaluation and tenant identification checks add minimum 10ms overhead at p95.
Shared resource contention and RLS policy evaluation under load cause p99 latency spikes of at least 50ms.
Consolidated database recovery affects all tenants simultaneously, requiring at least 30 minutes for coordinated restoration.
Cost-effective consolidated backup strategies use 15-minute intervals to balance expense and data loss tolerance.
Consolidated resource pools with quota enforcement and RLS overhead scale efficiently up to 5M jobs per hour.
Shared database with row-level security filtering supports up to 50K read QPS before connection pooling and RLS overhead degrades performance.
Consolidated write path with tenant quotas and shared transaction log supports up to 10K write QPS before contention limits scalability.
Pattern supports both audited and non-audited deployments, though audit logging is recommended for multi-tenant compliance.
Pattern specifically supports shared database with row-level security or schema-per-tenant isolation for cost-efficient consolidation.
Consolidated architecture handles both PII and non-PII data through row-level security and tenant-scoped access controls.
Pattern supports GDPR compliance through tenant-level data isolation, deletion capabilities, and access controls in shared infrastructure.
Pattern can support HIPAA requirements when combined with appropriate encryption, audit logging, and tenant isolation controls.
CCPA requirements for data access and deletion are achievable through tenant-scoped queries and RLS-enforced data boundaries.
SOX compliance is supported through proper access controls, audit trails, and tenant-isolated financial data in shared database.
Shared database architecture supports both eventual and read-your-writes consistency models depending on application requirements.
Pattern supports both strict and relaxed durability guarantees based on database configuration and cost-performance tradeoffs.

supports_constraints.reason: Multi-tenant consolidation works on any cloud platform or on-premises environment that supports database row-level security.
Architectural pattern for resource consolidation and tenant isolation applies across all programming languages.
Tenant consolidation is a cross-cutting architectural concern applicable to any platform type requiring multi-tenancy.
Pattern is specifically designed for multi-tenant architectures and requires multi-tenancy feature to be enabled.
Pattern works with or without caching layers, though tenant-aware caching can improve performance in consolidated environments.
Consolidation pattern applies to both synchronous and asynchronous workloads with tenant-scoped message routing.
Pattern supports AI inference workloads through shared compute pools with tenant quotas and resource isolation.
Batch processing benefits from consolidated resource pools with tenant-scoped job scheduling and quota enforcement.
Pattern supports distributed transactions within and across tenants using shared transaction coordination in consolidated database.
Real-time streaming workloads can leverage consolidated infrastructure with tenant-partitioned streams and quota controls.
Vector search operations benefit from shared index infrastructure with row-level security for tenant isolation.
Document stores support consolidation through tenant-scoped collections or RLS policies on shared document tables.
Key-value stores achieve consolidation through tenant-prefixed keys in shared data structures with quota enforcement.
Graph databases support multi-tenant consolidation through tenant node properties and edge-level access control policies.
Time-series databases benefit from consolidated storage with tenant tags for efficient querying and retention policies.
OLTP workloads achieve cost efficiency through shared transaction processing infrastructure with tenant-level isolation.
OLAP workloads benefit from consolidated analytical compute pools with tenant-scoped queries and resource quotas.

resource_sharing_level.description: Level of resource consolidation: infrastructure (shared VMs/containers), database (shared DB with RLS), application (shared app instances with tenant context), or hybrid (mix of approaches)

isolation_mechanism.description: Data isolation strategy: RLS (row-level security for maximum consolidation), schema_per_tenant (moderate isolation), database_per_tenant (strong isolation, higher cost), or application_logic (app-enforced filtering)

quota_enforcement.description: Where to enforce tenant quotas: database (DB-level limits), application (app-level metering), api_gateway (request throttling), or none (trust-based)

compute_pooling.description: Compute resource allocation: shared (all tenants share pool, lowest cost), tiered (premium tenants get dedicated resources), dedicated (per-tenant compute, highest cost)

storage_strategy.description: Database storage approach: shared_schema (single schema with tenant_id, most efficient), shared_table_partitioned (partitioned by tenant for performance), separate_schemas (schema per tenant), separate_databases (full isolation)

warn_nfr.reason: Tenant consolidation overhead (RLS evaluation, tenant identification) is not justified at very low QPS; simpler single-tenant or schema-per-DB approaches are more appropriate.
warn_nfr.message: arch-multi-tenant-consolidation: peak read QPS is {actual} req/s (<100). Consolidation overhead (RLS, tenant tracking) may not be cost-effective at this scale.

warn_nfr.reason: Pattern explicitly supports max 99.9% availability due to shared database single points of failure. Higher availability targets require database-per-tenant isolation.
warn_nfr.message: arch-multi-tenant-consolidation: availability target is {actual} (>99.9%). This pattern's shared database architecture limits maximum availability to 99.9% due to consolidated failure domains.

warn_nfr.reason: Database-per-tenant isolation contradicts the consolidation cost optimization goal of this pattern; use multi-tenant-database-per-tenant pattern instead.
warn_nfr.message: arch-multi-tenant-consolidation: spec requires tenant_isolation=database-per-tenant, which contradicts this pattern's shared-resource consolidation approach. Consider multi-tenant-database-per-tenant pattern instead.
